beats.prompt: Quick warm-up — match each tool to where you already used it.

feedback.correct: Two tools you already own: the conditional points one way (L7), and a rare prior swamps strong evidence (L2). Now spot them in the wild.
hints: From L7: P(E|H) ≠ P(H|E).
From L2: a rare base rate can outweigh a very strong test.
Reliability of the witness is forward; what you want is backward, weighted by the base rate.

beats.prompt: A witness — tested to be 80% reliable in the dark — says the hit-and-run cab was blue. But 85% of the city's cabs are green. How likely is the cab actually blue?

options: Under half — most cabs are green
About 15% — that's the blue base rate

About 80% — the witness is 80% reliable.note: That's P(says blue | blue), not P(blue | says blue). Of 100 cabs the witness calls 'blue' on 12 of the 15 blue AND 17 of the 85 green → 12/29 ≈ 41%.

Under half — most cabs are green.note: Right — the 85% green base rate produces 17 false 'blue' calls, swamping the 12 true ones → 12/29.

About 15% — that's the blue base rate.note: 15% is the prior, before the witness. The 80% testimony lifts it — but only to 12/29, not all the way to 80%.

hints: No wrong guess — we'll count 100 cabs.
80% is P(says blue | blue), not P(blue | says blue).
12 true 'blue' vs 17 false 'blue' → 12/29.

beats.prompt: Read this contrast — the number you're handed vs the number you look up — then continue.

interaction.title: The number you look up
interaction.body: Every real judgment has a number someone hands you (a witness's reliability, a test's accuracy, a model's recall) and a number you have to go look up (how common the thing is — the base rate). Base-rate neglect is forgetting the second. The fix is one habit: name the base rate, then update.

feedback.correct: Two numbers: the one they hand you (the witness's 80%) and the one you must look up (the 15% base rate). Real judgments hide the second.
hints: The reliability is given; the base rate is not — you fetch it.
P(says blue | blue) is handed to you; P(blue) you look up.
Name the base rate, then update.

beats.prompt: Drag the green/blue cab mix on a 100-cab array — watch false 'blue' calls swamp the true ones. PPV = 12/29.

hero.structuralReadout: Of 100 cabs the witness calls 'blue' on 12 of 15 blue and 17 of 85 green → 12 of 29 → 12/29.

feedback.correct: Hold the witness at 80% and drag the cab mix: at 15% blue, the 85 green cabs yield 17 false 'blue' calls — more than the 12 true ones. PPV = 12/(12+17) = 12/29.
hints: Drag the green/blue mix and watch the false-'blue' column.
Rare blue (15) × a reliable-ish witness still loses to 85 green × a 20% error.
12 true 'blue' vs 17 false 'blue' → 12/29.

beats.prompt: Of 100 cabs: 15 blue, 85 green; the witness is right 80% of the time. Compute P(actually blue | witness says blue).

feedback.correct: 12 true-blue calls / (12 true + 17 false) = 12/29 ≈ 41% — under half. The 80% is P(says blue | blue), not P(blue | says blue).
hints: Of 100 cabs: 15 blue, 85 green. The witness is right 80% of the time either way.

beats.prompt: Same move, ML costume: 20% of mail is spam, a filter has 99% recall and 2% false-positive rate. A message is flagged. What is P(spam | flagged)?
beats.interviewNote: PPV / precision under class imbalance is the canonical ML/quant base-rate question (interview pack `spam-precision`); the taxicab is its psychology classic (`showcase-taxicab`). The one-liner: a flag's precision is prior odds × likelihood ratio — recall (sensitivity) alone tells you nothing about precision until you weigh the base rate.

feedback.correct: Same move, ML costume: of 100 emails, 20 spam → 99% recall ≈ 19.8 caught; 80 ham × 2% ≈ 1.6 false flags → precision = 198/(198+16) = 99/107 ≈ 93%. (Recall 99% ≠ precision.)
hints: Recall is P(flagged | spam); you want precision P(spam | flagged).
Per 10,000: 2000 spam × 99% = 1980 true flags; 8000 ham × 2% = 160 false flags.

beats.prompt: Match each unlabeled scenario to its base-rate lesson — four costumes, one rule.

pairs.left: Fair vs two-headed coin, one head

feedback.correct: Four costumes, one rule: name the base rate, weight the evidence, renormalize. Only the disguise changes.
hints: For each, ask: what's the prior (base rate), and how strong is the evidence?
A rare base rate (disease, fraud) drags a strong test down; a common one (no rare prior) doesn't.

beats.prompt: Three methods all converge on 12/29 — reveal each lens.

lenses.label: Frequencies
lenses.body: Of 100 cabs: 12 true-blue calls vs 17 false-blue calls → 12 of 29

feedback.correct: The formula, the 12-vs-17 frequency count, and the odds ratio all give 12/29 — not a quirk of these numbers.
hints: Reveal each lens.
They are three roads to the same number.
All three say 12/29.

beats.prompt: Transfer: an email spam filter has 99% recall and a 2% false-positive rate; spam makes up 1 in every 200 messages. The filter flags a message. What is P(spam | flagged)?

interaction.scenario: A spam filter with 99% recall and a 2% false-positive rate flags a message; spam is 1 in every 200 messages.

feedback.correct: Same natural-frequency count as the fraud model, with email in place of transactions: \[ P(\text{spam}\mid\text{flagged}) = \frac{P(\text{spam})\,P(\text{flagged}\mid\text{spam})}{P(\text{spam})\,P(\text{flagged}\mid\text{spam}) + P(\text{ham})\,P(\text{flagged}\mid\text{ham})} \] Per 100,000 messages (spam rate \(= \tfrac{1}{200}\)): 500 spam \(\times\,99\% = 495\) true flags; 99,500 ham \(\times\,2\% = 1990\) false flags; total flagged \(= 2485\). Substituting: \[ P(\text{spam}\mid\text{flagged}) = \frac{495}{2485} = \frac{99}{497} \approx 20\% \] Even 99% recall cannot rescue a flag when ham outnumbers spam 199 to 1 — the 2% false-positive rate on a huge ham pool produces four false flags for every true one.
hints: Recall 99% is \(P(\text{flagged}\mid\text{spam})\); you want \(P(\text{spam}\mid\text{flagged})\) — flip the arrow using the 1-in-200 base rate.
Per 100,000 messages: 500 spam \(\times\,99\% = 495\) true flags; 99,500 ham \(\times\,2\% = 1990\) false flags; total 2,485.

beats.prompt: Mastery challenge: a fraud model with 95% recall and a 1% false-positive rate flags a transaction; fraud occurs in 1 of every 1000 transactions. What is P(fraud | flagged)?

interaction.scenario: A fraud model with 95% recall and a 1% false-positive rate flags a transaction; fraud occurs in 1 of every 1,000 transactions.

feedback.correct: Apply Bayes with a natural-frequency count: \[ P(\text{fraud}\mid\text{flagged}) = \frac{P(\text{fraud})\,P(\text{flagged}\mid\text{fraud})}{P(\text{fraud})\,P(\text{flagged}\mid\text{fraud}) + P(\text{honest})\,P(\text{flagged}\mid\text{honest})} \] Per 100,000 transactions (fraud rate \(= \tfrac{1}{1000}\)): 100 fraud \(\times\,95\% = 95\) true flags; 99,900 honest \(\times\,1\% = 999\) false flags; total flagged \(= 1094\). Substituting: \[ P(\text{fraud}\mid\text{flagged}) = \frac{95}{1094} \approx 8.7\% \] A 95% recall model on a 1-in-1,000 base rate still produces roughly 10 false alarms per true flag — the rare prior dominates.
hints: Recall 95% is \(P(\text{flagged}\mid\text{fraud})\); you want \(P(\text{fraud}\mid\text{flagged})\) — flip the arrow using the base rate.
Per 100,000 transactions: 100 fraud \(\times\,95\% = 95\) true flags; 99,900 honest \(\times\,1\% = 999\) false flags.

beats.prompt: Reveal the concept-capstone recap — the through-line of all eight lessons.

feedback.correct: Real judgments hide the base rate — the number you have to look up. Name the base rate, then update: weight the evidence by how well each hypothesis predicted it, and renormalize. Same rule, every costume.
hints: A reliable witness, a sharp test, a high-recall model — none beats a rare enough base rate (12/29, 1/2, 95/1094).
P(says blue | blue) ≠ P(blue | says blue); recall ≠ precision; accuracy ≠ P(hypothesis).
That closes Bayes' Rule: evidence rescales belief in proportion to how well each hypothesis predicted it.
